original_abstract: The speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT) is one of the most pervasive phenomena in behavioral science, characterizing choice behavior across a vast taxonomic range—from insects and rodents to primates. The principle that decision speed and accuracy are inextricably linked is a fundamental property of goal-directed action. In recent years, the SAT has seen a resurgence of interest as neuroscientific techniques begin to map its physiological substrates and computational models adopt it as a pivotol benchmark for validity. This review provides a comprehensive examination of the SAT through three lenses. First, I trace its historical development as an empirical window into the "velocity of thought" and its role in shaping mathematical models of sequential information sampling. Second, I offer a practical "users guide" to SAT methodology, critically evaluating experimental manipulations—such as response deadlines, payoffs, and titration—alongside the analysis of behavioral signatures like conditional accuracy functions and quantile-probability plots. Finally, I review the application of these tools across diverse domains, including neurophysiology, pharmacology, and the study of cognitive pathology.
humanized_abstract: choice at the speed a animal can move is a universal principle of behaviour that has been observed from the simplest insects and rodents, to even our closest relatives, the primates. The fact that this speed–accuracy trade-off (SAT) governs such a wide range of goal directed movements is thus a very general principle of behavior. More recently, advances in neuroscience are beginning to elucidate the brain mechanisms underlying SAT behaviour, while computational theories are being challenged for validity in light of the now well acknowledged importance of this principle. This review describes choice behaviour in relation to speed in three ways. Firstly, the work that has given rise to this current reinterest in the SAT is reviewed: the development of the idea as a probe of what can be termed velocity of thought and its impact upon the increasing popular models of how animals ‘sample’ along a decision path. Secondly, a practical users guide for researchers working at the SAT level is outlined by describing all known manipulations (deadlines, pay and titration for choice behaviour as well as methodologies for the identification of key performance characteristics of the SAT e.g. the conditional accuracy function and the use of quantile–probability plots). Finally, the implications of such experiments into the vast number of on-going studies are reviewed within the context of neuroscience, pharmacology and abnormal cognition.
